title: The Death and Life of Great American Cities
excerpt: A revolutionary critique of 20th-century urban planning that champions the wisdom of dense, mixed-use neighborhoods over top-down modernist redevelopment, forever changing how we think about cities.
keyIdeas: Cities are complex ecosystems that cannot be understood through simplistic planning models
Mixed-use neighborhoods with dense, diverse populations create vibrant, safe, economically productive urban environments
The 'eyes on the street' principle: active, diverse street life creates natural surveillance and public safety
Top-down modernist planning (Le Corbusier, Howard, Burnham) destroys the organic fabric that makes cities work
keyTakeaways: Successful cities are organized at the neighborhood level through complex, informal systems of mutual support and daily interaction
Four conditions generate urban diversity: mixed primary uses, short blocks, aged buildings, and dense concentrations of people
The Garden City movement and urban renewal programs harmed cities by segregating uses and eliminating street-level activity
Planners should study how successful city neighborhoods actually work rather than imposing abstract design principles
whoShouldRead: Urban planners, architects, and city officials — the book is the foundational critique of 20th-century planning orthodoxy
Community activists and neighborhood organizers fighting bad development proposals
Anyone who lives in a city and wonders why some neighborhoods thrive while others decline
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for a systematic planning textbook — this is a polemic, not a comprehensive guide
Those who believe in top-down, visionary urban redevelopment on the model of Le Corbusier or Robert Moses
Anyone seeking technical guidance on transportation, infrastructure, or zoning law
